spoken_quote: red and green motley
paraphrase: Aerys sent Steffon Baratheon across the narrow sea to find a bride for Prince Rhaegar; he didn't find one, but found a remarkable young slave boy with a tattooed face, who could juggle, joke, and sing in four languages, and decided to bring him home for his family.
verbatim: No one ever explained those two days the fool had been lost in the sea. The fisherfolk liked to say a mermaid had taught him to breathe water in return for his seed. Patchface himself had said nothing. The witty, clever lad that Lord Steffon had written of never reached Storm’s End; the boy they found was someone else, broken in body and mind, hardly capable of speech, much less of wit. Yet his fool’s face left no doubt of who he was. It was the fashion in the Free City of Volantis to tattoo the faces of slaves and servants; from neck to scalp the boy’s skin had been patterned in squares of red and green motley.

paraphrase: On the way back, Steffon's ship was smashed in a storm; Steffon, Cassana, and a hundred men drowned, and corpses washed up at Storm's End for days afterward.

spoken_quote: broken in body and mind
paraphrase: On the third day, Patchface washed up naked, sandy, and cold, seeming dead - then woke up alive, though 'broken in body and mind.'
verbatim: No one ever explained those two days the fool had been lost in the sea. The fisherfolk liked to say a mermaid had taught him to breathe water in return for his seed. Patchface himself had said nothing. The witty, clever lad that Lord Steffon had written of never reached Storm’s End; the boy they found was someone else, broken in body and mind, hardly capable of speech, much less of wit. Yet his fool’s face left no doubt of who he was. It was the fashion in the Free City of Volantis to tattoo the faces of slaves and servants; from neck to scalp the boy’s skin had been patterned in squares of red and green motley.

spoken_quote: mad
paraphrase: Patchface was no longer bright and clever - he now seemed 'mad,' talking nonsense, twitching, and trembling; some wanted to put him out of his misery, but Maester Cressen spared him, though he doubts Patchface is even glad to be alive.
By Book 2, Patchface lives on Dragonstone with Shireen, a sad and lonely girl; they form a sweet friendship and she calls him 'Patches.'

spoken_quote: Under the sea, the fish eat us
paraphrase: Patchface's songs describe 'under the sea' as an opposite-world to life on land - e.g. 'Here we eat fish,' but 'under the sea, the fish eat us.'
verbatim: Patchface was capering about as the maester made his slow way around the table to Davos Seaworth. “Here we eat fish,” the fool declared happily, waving a cod about like a scepter. “Under the sea, the fish eat us. I know, I know, oh, oh, oh.”

spoken_quote: Under the sea, you fall up
paraphrase: Patchface falls down and says 'Under the sea, you fall up,' which may refer to how corpses float, tying the sea to death in his songs.
verbatim: Patchface sprawled half on top of him, motley fool’s face pressed close to his own. He had lost his tin helm with its antlers and bells. “Under the sea, you fall up,” he declared. “I know, I know, oh, oh, oh.” Giggling, the fool rolled off, bounded to his feet, and did a little dance.

spoken_quote: Fool's blood, king's blood, blood on the maiden's thigh, but chains for the guests and chains for the bridegroom, aye aye aye
paraphrase: In Book 3, Patchface sings a song about 'fool's blood, king's blood, blood on the maiden's thigh... chains for the guests and chains for the bridegroom,' which is read as an accurate prediction of the Red Wedding: Aegon Frey (fool's blood), Robb Stark (king's blood), Roslin Frey's deflowering (blood on the maiden's thigh), and Edmure/other imprisoned survivors (the chains).
verbatim: When the fool saw Davos, he jerked to a sudden halt, the bells on his antlered tin helmet going ting-a-ling, ting-a-ling. Hopping from one foot to the other, he sang, “Fool’s blood, king’s blood, blood on the maiden’s thigh, but chains for the guests and chains for the bridegroom, aye aye aye.” Shireen almost caught him then, but at the last instant he hopped over a patch of bracken and vanished among the trees. The princess was right behind him. The sight of them made Davos smile.

spoken_quote: The merwives wear nennymoans in their hair and weave gowns of silver seaweed
paraphrase: Patchface sings that 'the merwives wear nennymoans in their hair and weave gowns of silver seaweed'; at Joffrey's wedding Sansa wears a silver gown with the poison used to kill Joffrey hidden in her hair, so this may be Patchface's vision of Joffrey's death.
verbatim: Patchface rang his bells. “It is always summer under the sea,” he intoned. “The merwives wear nennymoans in their hair and weave gowns of silver seaweed. I know, I know, oh, oh, oh.”

spoken_quote: Under the sea, smoke rises in bubbles, and flames burn green and blue and black
paraphrase: In a Davos chapter, Patches sings 'under the sea, smoke rises in bubbles, and flames burn green and blue and black,' which may warn of the Battle of the Blackwater, where green wildfire burns amid black smoke and blue sea.
verbatim: Stannis Baratheon strode forward like a soldier marching into battle. His squires stepped up to attend him. Davos watched as his son Devan pulled a long padded glove over the king’s right hand. The boy wore a cream-colored doublet with a fiery heart sewn on the breast. Bryen Farring was similarly garbed as he tied a stiff leather cape around His Grace’s neck. Behind, Davos heard a faint clank and clatter of bells. “Under the sea, smoke rises in bubbles, and flames burn green and blue and black,” Patchface sang somewhere. “I know, I know, oh, oh, oh.”

spoken_quote: The shadows come to dance
paraphrase: Patchface often sings 'the shadows come to dance,' which may refer to the shadow-monster assassins Melisandre uses to kill people.

spoken_quote: The shadows come to stay
paraphrase: Patchface also sings 'the shadows come to stay,' which may instead refer to the white walkers, who are described as 'shadows' in the books.

spoken_quote: In the dark the dead are dancing
paraphrase: Patchface sings 'in the dark the dead are dancing,' referring to the white walkers' army of the dead.
verbatim: “In the dark the dead are dancing.” Patchface shuffled his feet in a grotesque dance step. “I know, I know, oh oh oh.” At Eastwatch someone had sewn him a motley cloak of beaver pelts, sheepskins, and rabbit fur. His hat sported antlers hung with bells and long brown flaps of squirrel fur that hung down over his ears. Every step he took set him to ringing.

spoken_quote: No one ever explained those two days the fool had been lost in the sea
paraphrase: It's a mystery how Patchface survived the shipwreck that killed everyone else aboard - the text says no one ever explained the two days the fool had been lost at sea.
verbatim: No one ever explained those two days the fool had been lost in the sea. The fisherfolk liked to say a mermaid had taught him to breathe water in return for his seed. Patchface himself had said nothing. The witty, clever lad that Lord Steffon had written of never reached Storm’s End; the boy they found was someone else, broken in body and mind, hardly capable of speech, much less of wit. Yet his fool’s face left no doubt of who he was. It was the fashion in the Free City of Volantis to tattoo the faces of slaves and servants; from neck to scalp the boy’s skin had been patterned in squares of red and green motley.

spoken_quote: coughed water and sat up
paraphrase: When Patchface washed up his flesh was cold and he seemed dead, until he 'coughed water and sat up.'
verbatim: The boy washed up on the third day. Maester Cressen had come down with the rest, to help put names to the dead. When they found the fool he was naked, his skin white and wrinkled and powdered with wet sand. Cressen had thought him another corpse, but when Jommy grabbed his ankles to drag him off to the burial wagon, the boy coughed water and sat up. To his dying day, Jommy had sworn that Patchface’s flesh was clammy cold.

paraphrase: This kind of revival after drowning is similar to the ironborn drowning ritual, in which believers of the Drowned God are deliberately drowned and then revived as a show of faith.

spoken_quote: the Drowned God's watery halls
paraphrase: Ironborn believe that after death they go to 'the Drowned God's watery halls' to 'feast' with 'mermaids' 'for all eternity.'
Patchface's songs describe feasting with mermen under the sea, matching the ironborn's underwater afterlife beliefs.

spoken_quote: the Drowned God's watery halls
paraphrase: Patchface mentions mermaids blowing seashells like trumpets; Asha Greyjoy says the same thing, that merlings blow seashells in 'the Drowned God's watery halls.'

spoken_quote: the Great Other
paraphrase: Gods in the series give undead champions: Beric Dondarrion is resurrected by R'hllor's fire magic, Bloodraven and Coldhands serve the old gods, and white walkers raise wights to serve the 'Great Other' - so Patchface may be a 'water zombie' raised to serve the Drowned God.
verbatim: “The war,” she affirmed. “There are two, Onion Knight. Not seven, not one, not a hundred or a thousand. Two! Do you think I crossed half the world to put yet another vain king on yet another empty throne? The war has been waged since time began, and before it is done, all men must choose where they will stand. On one side is R’hllor, the Lord of Light, the Heart of Fire, the God of Flame and Shadow. Against him stands the Great Other whose name may not be spoken, the Lord of Darkness, the Soul of Ice, the God of Night and Terror. Ours is not a choice between Baratheon and Lannister, between Greyjoy and Stark. It is death we choose, or life. Darkness, or light.” She clasped the bars of his cell with her slender white hands. The great ruby at her throat seemed to pulse with its own radiance. “So tell me, Ser Davos Seaworth, and tell me truly—does your heart burn with the shining light of R’hllor? Or is it black and cold and full of worms?” She reached through the bars and laid three fingers upon his breast, as if to feel the truth of him through flesh and wool and leather.

paraphrase: By contrast, Aeron 'Damphair' Greyjoy believes himself the Drowned God's prophet and claims to have died and seen the watery halls before being resurrected, but he's never made an actual prophecy or done anything magical - his belief seems to be a coping mechanism after childhood abuse, unlike Patchface, who was really drowned and reborn.

spoken_quote: half his wits and all his memory
paraphrase: Becoming divine costs part of one's humanity: Beric barely remembers his old life and Bloodraven is more tree than man; Patchface is the same, since the sea has taken 'half his wits and all his memory.'
verbatim: Even for a fool, Patchface was a sorry thing. Perhaps once he could evoke gales of laughter with a quip, but the sea had taken that power from him, along with half his wits and all his memory. He was soft and obese, subject to twitches and trembles, incoherent as often as not. The girl was the only one who laughed at him now, the only one who cared if he lived or died.

spoken_quote: Only death can pay for life
paraphrase: The series repeats that 'only death can pay for life' (e.g. Dany's unborn child sacrificed to raise Drogo); a hundred Baratheon men drowned alongside Patchface, and ironborn see drowning people as a sacrifice to the Drowned God, so these deaths may have paid for Patchface's resurrection.

spoken_quote: dangerous
paraphrase: Melisandre says Patchface is 'dangerous' - she sees him in visions with skulls about him and bloody red lips.

spoken_quote: demon
paraphrase: The red priest Moqorro says the Drowned God is a 'demon,' a thrall of the god of the white walkers, suggesting Patchface could be on the side of the dead.

spoken_quote: We will march into the sea and out again
paraphrase: In Book 5, when Jon Snow plans the mission to Hardhome and someone asks who'll lead it, Patchface volunteers, saying he will lead it and 'we will march into the sea and out again' - which could mean dying and then rising again like the dead at Hardhome.
verbatim: Patchface jumped up. “I will lead it!” His bells rang merrily. “We will march into the sea and out again. Under the waves we will ride seahorses, and mermaids will blow seashells to announce our coming, oh, oh, oh.”

spoken_quote: mermaids will blow seashells to announce our coming
paraphrase: Patchface also says 'mermaids will blow seashells to announce our coming,' paralleling how the Night's Watch blows horns to announce white walker attacks.
verbatim: Patchface jumped up. “I will lead it!” His bells rang merrily. “We will march into the sea and out again. Under the waves we will ride seahorses, and mermaids will blow seashells to announce our coming, oh, oh, oh.”

spoken_quote: Dead things in the woods
paraphrase: The Hardhome massacre hasn't actually happened in the books yet - we've only heard reports of 'dead things in the woods' and 'dead things in the water' - so it's unconfirmed whether Patchface will play any role there.
Patchface's use of the words 'we' and 'our' when talking about marching into the sea suggests he identifies with the side of the dead, just as Melisandre and Moqorro warn.
George R.R. Martin is a fan of H.P. Lovecraft, and there are references to Lovecraft throughout the series, including ancient ruined eastern cities named K'dath, Carcosa, and Leng.

spoken_quote: squishers
paraphrase: The books mention 'squishers,' half-fish creatures that breed with humans, and there are Westerosi families with webbed fingers and bulging eyes, resembling the fish-human hybrids of Lovecraft's Deep Ones.
It's said the Deep Ones built structures in Westeros, including the ironborn's Seastone Chair, and that the Drowned God is the father of the Deep Ones.

spoken_quote: to breathe water in return for his seed
paraphrase: After Patchface drowned, fisherfolk gossiped that a mermaid had taught him to breathe water in return for his seed, tying him to fish-human breeding lore.
verbatim: No one ever explained those two days the fool had been lost in the sea. The fisherfolk liked to say a mermaid had taught him to breathe water in return for his seed. Patchface himself had said nothing. The witty, clever lad that Lord Steffon had written of never reached Storm’s End; the boy they found was someone else, broken in body and mind, hardly capable of speech, much less of wit. Yet his fool’s face left no doubt of who he was. It was the fashion in the Free City of Volantis to tattoo the faces of slaves and servants; from neck to scalp the boy’s skin had been patterned in squares of red and green motley.

spoken_quote: Under the sea, men marry fishes
paraphrase: Patchface sings 'under the sea, men marry fishes,' further hinting at human-fish interbreeding.
verbatim: “Under the sea, men marry fishes.” Patchface did a little dance step, jingling his bells. “They do, they do, they do.”

spoken_quote: Come with me beneath the sea, away, away, away
paraphrase: In Book 5, Patchface takes Shireen by the hand and sings 'come with me beneath the sea, away, away, away,' raising the possibility he could take her to the Deep Ones.
verbatim: The bells on his hat rang. “Away, away,” the fool sang. “Come with me beneath the sea, away, away, away.” He took the little princess by one hand and drew her from the room, skipping.

paraphrase: As a baby, Shireen caught greyscale, an infectious disease that turns the skin grey and hard, eventually causes madness and death (turning victims into stone men); children can survive, and Shireen did, left with just a mark on her face.

spoken_quote: not clean
paraphrase: In Book 5, the wildling Val says Shireen is 'not clean' - that her greyscale is only dormant and will 'wake again,' raising fear she could infect others.
Greyscale is associated with damp, cold climates, and is said to have been a curse inflicted by the Rhoynar Prince Garin, as a form of water magic.

spoken_quote: risen from his watery grave
paraphrase: Some say Garin has 'risen from his watery grave' to become the Shrouded Lord, a figure who spreads greyscale with a 'grey kiss.'
verbatim: “The Shrouded Lord has ruled these mists since Garin’s day,” said Yandry. “Some say that he himself is Garin, risen from his watery grave.”

paraphrase: Patchface parallels the Shrouded Lord/Garin - both connected to water magic and having risen from a 'watery grave' - and Melisandre already sees skulls about him, so if Shireen's greyscale wakes, Patchface (e.g. if he turns violent when Melisandre tries to burn his only friend) could help spread it into a plague, possibly fulfilling Melisandre's prophecy of waking a stone dragon.
This ties back to Shireen, who has dreams of being eaten by stone dragons.

spoken_quote: Old powers waken. Shadows stir. An age of wonder and terror will soon be upon us
paraphrase: The video closes on the line 'old powers waken, shadows stir, an age of wonder and terror will soon be upon us' as a description of what The Winds of Winter will bring.
